current_fact.fact_text: Beneath the headlines of 1912, the Titanic carried a remarkable cross-section of society—from famous tycoons to entire families traveling under assumed names. One lesser-known passenger, violinist Wallace Hartley, had his instrument recovered from the icy water and returned to his fiancée; a century later, that same violin was auctioned for nearly a million dollars as a sonic relic of the disaster.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

current_fact.match_reason: A single violin carrying the emotional memory of a catastrophe echoes the way Sedna’s first ever episode became a keepsake soundtrack for the station’s own origin story.

queue.fact_text: The Cambodian Civil War’s April 15, 1970 massacres weren’t just isolated atrocities—they foreshadowed the brutal ethnic cleansing policies that would intensify under the Khmer Rouge. The Mekong River, a lifeline for millions, became an unwitting witness, carrying bodies past villages that suddenly had to decide whether to look away or remember.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A river transformed into a dark current of history contrasts with an episode that invites us to immerse more consciously in water and feeling, suggesting a different kind of depth.

queue.fact_text: Ella Baker’s 1960 SNCC meeting at Shaw University was radical not just for what it created, but for what it rejected: she openly criticized the “messiah” model of civil rights leadership. Instead, she championed slow, patient organizing where ordinary people discovered their own power step by step.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Both Baker’s philosophy and the episode celebrate slow, collective learning over heroic shortcuts, honoring persistence as a quiet form of strength.

queue.fact_text: When Jackie Robinson took his first Major League at-bat on April 15, 1947, the Dodgers’ manager quietly moved him from his natural position at second base to first—partly to ease him into the big leagues, partly to keep him literally at the center of the game. It was a small tactical choice that made his barrier-breaking debut even more visible.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A Black athlete stepping into hostile territory and claiming space in the infield parallels women claiming space on the dancefloor as a cultural and political act.

published.fact_text: In the early hours of April 15, 1912, the supposedly “unsinkable” RMS Titanic disappeared beneath the North Atlantic after striking an iceberg, taking over 1,500 lives with her. Among the lesser-known details: the ship’s band kept playing to calm passengers, and wireless radio—still a relatively new technology—helped nearby ships rescue the 710 survivors.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: An episode about cold northern seas and deep water encounters mirrors the icy Atlantic setting and reflective mood of the Titanic’s final night.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1738, George Frideric Handel’s opera "Serse" premiered in London—and flopped so badly it closed after just five performances. Ironically, its opening aria "Ombra mai fù" later became one of the most famous and frequently performed pieces in all of opera, outliving the work’s original failure by centuries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode explore how vocal traditions and once-marginal works transform over time into canonical, emotionally powerful music.

published.fact_text: The EC-121 shootdown on April 15, 1969, saw a U.S. Navy signals-intelligence aircraft destroyed by North Korean fighters over the Sea of Japan, killing all 31 crew. The mission was unarmed and flying in international airspace, turning a routine reconnaissance flight into a Cold War flashpoint that nearly triggered major escalation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A tense Cold War incident and an episode centered on political rage and militarized power both spotlight the human cost of geopolitical confrontation.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1920, a payroll robbery in South Braintree, Massachusetts left two security guards dead and eventually led to the infamous Sacco and Vanzetti trial. The case became a global symbol of how fear of anarchism and prejudice against immigrants could bend the idea of justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A story about criminal trials, political fear, and marginalized identities pairs with an episode focused on an artist who turns injustice and identity struggle into pointed social critique.

published.fact_text: The Hillsborough disaster on April 15, 1989, began as an FA Cup semi-final and became Britain’s worst sports stadium tragedy when overcrowding caused a fatal crush that killed 97 Liverpool fans. The disaster exposed deep failures in crowd management and policing, and it took families decades of campaigning to overturn early, misleading narratives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: The episode is a sonic portrait of Sheffield, the city whose club hosted the match, offering a cultural counterpoint to a day marked by grief and community resilience.

published.fact_text: During the Cambodian Civil War, on April 15, 1970, anti-Vietnamese massacres sent hundreds of bodies drifting down the Mekong River into South Vietnam. This haunting image captures how ethnic violence can literally flow across borders, leaving scars on both landscapes and communities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An episode about music born from exile and ethnic persecution echoes the story of a persecuted minority whose suffering flowed silently along a great river.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1952, the Boeing B-52 Stratofortress took to the skies for the first time, debuting a bomber design so durable that some airframes are expected to serve for nearly a century. Powered by eight engines and designed in the early Jet Age, the B-52 has quietly outlived many of the engineers who built it.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The roaring, electrified power of guitar-driven rock echoes the thunderous, high-energy presence of an eight-engine jet bomber.

published.fact_text: In the night of April 15, 1941, around 200 Luftwaffe bombers turned Belfast into a firestorm, killing roughly 1,000 people in the city’s worst World War II air raid. Many residents had assumed their remote location would spare them and had few air-raid shelters, a deadly miscalculation that reshaped local memory of the war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An episode about weathering storms and rebuilding after dark seasons resonates with a city that survived a devastating rain of bombs.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1922, a Wyoming senator’s seemingly dry resolution to probe a secret oil lease at a place called Teapot Dome cracked open one of the biggest political scandals in U.S. history. The investigation revealed that public oil reserves had been quietly handed to private companies in exchange for bribes paid in cash, bonds, and even livestock.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Both the scandal and the episode question how invisible systems—whether political deals or digital networks—quietly shape power and everyday life.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1736, the short-lived Kingdom of Corsica was proclaimed by adventurer Theodor von Neuhoff, who arrived by ship with a cargo of weapons and declared himself king. His monarchy lasted mere months, a curious reminder of how fragile and improvised early modern “kingdoms” could be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A tiny Mediterranean kingdom born of improvisation pairs with an episode about Italian landscapes and seafaring songs, blurring borders between history, travel, and myth.

published.fact_text: The Pocotaligo Massacre on April 15, 1715, ignited the Yamasee War, one of the most serious Native American uprisings against British colonists in the early American South. It erupted over trade abuses and land pressure, showing how fragile colonial alliances could flip into full-scale war almost overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An Indigenous-led resistance against colonial exploitation echoes the Tuareg’s own history of defending land and autonomy against outside control.

published.fact_text: At the Battle of Rain on April 15, 1632, Swedish king Gustavus Adolphus outmaneuvered the Imperial army by crossing a river under fire and attacking their flank. His innovative use of mobile artillery and coordinated infantry-fire tactics helped reshape European warfare toward a more modern style.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A king who rewired battlefield tactics matches with an episode about musicians who rewired rock’s rules—both stories of daring innovations against entrenched power.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1071, the Byzantine-held city of Bari surrendered to Norman leader Robert Guiscard, ending nearly five centuries of Byzantine rule in southern Italy. It marked a quiet but decisive shift in who controlled the "hinge" between the Latin West and the Greek East.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

published.match_reason: A turning point in the story of southern Italy finds a gentle mirror in an episode about Italian islands, identity, and how geography shapes culture and belonging.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 2021, a gunman opened fire at a FedEx Ground facility in Indianapolis, killing nine people and injuring seven more in a matter of minutes. The attack highlighted how workplaces—spaces meant for routine and stability—can be abruptly transformed into sites of trauma.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

published.match_reason: The need for small, gentle spaces to breathe and "unfreeze" emotionally fits the aftermath of a senseless workplace tragedy that leaves a community in shock.

published.fact_text: When fire broke out at Notre-Dame de Paris on April 15, 2019, it took fewer than 30 minutes for the spire to collapse, yet firefighters managed to save the main structure and many relics. The blaze triggered a global wave of donations and debates, turning a near-loss into a conversation about how we restore shared heritage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A wounded cathedral and an episode about healing after a sudden break both explore how something beloved can be rebuilt slowly, with care and collective effort.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1986, U.S. warplanes launched Operation El Dorado Canyon, striking targets in Libya in retaliation for a bombing at a West Berlin discotheque frequented by American soldiers. The operation used mid-air refueling and complex routes to avoid unfriendly airspace, turning a nightclub attack into a multi-continent military choreography.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A raid over Libya, sparked by a discotheque bombing, resonates with an episode that sonically wanders North African streets and night-time rhythms, but reclaims them for joy instead of violence.

published.fact_text: At Shaw University on April 15, 1960, grassroots organizer Ella Baker helped students form the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC). Rather than centering famous leaders, SNCC empowered young people to lead sit-ins, Freedom Rides, and voter-registration drives across the American South.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: An organizer who amplified young, often female, voices in the civil rights movement pairs with an episode celebrating women using hip hop as their own tool of resistance.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1947, Jackie Robinson stepped onto the field for the Brooklyn Dodgers and quietly shattered Major League Baseball’s color line. Under intense racist hostility, he was instructed not to fight back for his first seasons, turning self-control itself into a radical act.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

published.match_reason: A trailblazing athlete who transformed his field aligns with an episode about evolution, courage, and refusing to stay in the role others assign you.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1923, an arsonist set fire to the Nihon Shōgakkō, a Japanese mission school in Sacramento, killing ten children in a racially motivated attack. It’s a stark, often overlooked chapter in the story of anti-Asian violence and exclusion on the west coast of the United States.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: In the face of a racist attack on children, an episode honoring powerful, nurturing voices offers a counterweight of care, strength, and protection from marginalized perspectives.

published.fact_text: On April 15, 1900, Filipino guerrillas besieged a U.S. garrison in the town of Catubig, keeping them pinned down for four days during the Philippine–American War. The siege showed how local fighters, using knowledge of terrain and surprise, could challenge a far better-equipped imperial army.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A story of a small nation pushing back against empire matches an episode about a genre born from marginalized Caribbean voices resisting cultural dominance and rewriting the mainstream.

published.fact_text: Beneath the headlines of 1912, the Titanic carried a remarkable cross-section of society—from famous tycoons to entire families traveling under assumed names. One lesser-known passenger, violinist Wallace Hartley, had his instrument recovered from the icy water and returned to his fiancée; a century later, that same violin was auctioned for nearly a million dollars as a sonic relic of the disaster.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A single violin carrying the emotional memory of a catastrophe echoes the way Sedna’s first ever episode became a keepsake soundtrack for the station’s own origin story.
